topic: Spontaneous Human Combustion and Forensic Fire Science
title: Spontaneous Human Combustion: Myth, Misinterpretation, and the Science Behind Mysterious Fire Deaths

lesson.learn:
Spontaneous human combustion sounds like a supernatural fire, but forensic science has shown that most reported cases do not require a body to ignite from within. In many incidents, the likely explanation is an ordinary ignition source combined with the wick effect: clothing or bedding catches fire, body fat fuels the burn, and the fire can seem unusually intense and localized. Because the room may not be destroyed in the same way a house fire would be, the scene can look far stranger than it really is.
The mystery remains compelling because the deaths are tragic and the visuals are shocking. But the label often describes an appearance, not a proven cause.
lesson.simpler: Most alleged spontaneous human combustion cases are better explained by an external fire source, body fat, and the wick effect than by a body igniting on its own.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The strangeness of spontaneous human combustion mostly comes from fire behavior and scene interpretation, not from evidence that human bodies can ignite themselves.

thirdImageCredit.creator: ESO/J. Emerson/VISTA. Acknowledgment: Cambridge Astronomical Survey Unit

lesson.deeperDive:
The wick effect works like a candle. A small flame can set clothing or fabric alight, then melted fat feeds the fire for a longer period than a casual observer might expect. Forensic investigators also look for smoking materials, electrical faults, open flames, or other ignition sources. Because the fire can concentrate around the body while leaving parts of the room relatively intact, early witnesses often jumped to extraordinary conclusions.
Modern fire science has made the topic far less mysterious than legend suggests. Burn patterns, chemical evidence, and scene reconstruction usually point to a normal ignition source. What survives is not a hidden human fire, but a lesson in how dramatic scenes can create myths when people do not yet understand the mechanics.
lesson.funFact: The wick effect can make a fire behave a little like a candle, which is why some scenes look much stranger than they are.
references: Forensic fire-science and burn-pattern analysis literature.
Reviews of alleged spontaneous combustion cases.
Investigations into the wick effect and fire scene reconstruction.

people: John DeHaan - Forensic fire investigator who explained the wick effect.
Fire investigators - They trace the ignition source and burn pattern.
Medical examiners - They help determine what caused the death.
Eyewitnesses - Their descriptions often fuel the legend.
places: Living rooms - Common sites in alleged cases.
Bedrooms - Places where smoking or lamps can start fires.
Fire laboratories - Where burn behavior is tested.
Crime scenes - Where investigators look for ordinary ignition sources.
years: 19th century - Alleged cases become part of popular folklore.
1950s - Scientific skepticism grows around the phenomenon.
1990s - Forensic work popularizes the wick effect explanation.
2020s - Fire science continues to treat the issue as an evidence problem.
concepts: Wick effect - Burning clothing and fat like a candle wick.
Ignition source - The initial heat or flame that starts a fire.
Burn pattern - Clues left by fire behavior at a scene.
Forensic reconstruction - Rebuilding what likely happened from evidence.
Combustion - The chemical process of burning.
